item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got to admit, I was expecting an addendum at the end that the researcher who'd suggested letting her go had been part of the CI in some way. Not disappointed, as such, but would have nailed it for me. Liked it anyway though, +1 :)

item.title: Absolutely Sure
item.content:encoded: If all of their tests, including [REDACTED] clear her, what will it take for the Foundation to be "absolutely sure" she isn't anomalous?

item.title: Maybe she isn't special, but is important?
item.content:encoded: Maybe they want her because someone or something that is anomalous requires her in specific in order to do something they want, or they somehow found they need her to stop something? Or maybe their actions are inspired by some prophecy that it requires her to be under their control, or maybe simply require her to not be free?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like a horror type response from the captured guy wouldve been better. no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ehhhh it just feels and reads like a poor man's version of scp-884 -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1943-2 has potential, but not with SCP-1943-1.

I like ones where you have to fill in the blanks yourself, but this one feels kinda like it's just giving you a blank page and telling you to be creative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. I suppose I partly really like it because of all the implications it holds about the CI and how seriously the Foundation is willing to take their assessment of this apparently wholly ordinary person. It could just be the CI trolling, but there's absolutely no way to be sure.

Sure, sure, the "normal OR IS IT" is an overdone trope in general, but this still works quite well.

EDIT: Also, it does make me think of 884, but 884 was actually deadly and not the kind of thing I can imagine the CI trying only once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like this but I'm not sure why this is an SCP. Until they actually find something anomalous about her she needs to be protected, not contained. And the CI operative corpse has nothing to do with her, so why is it being labeled as part of the same SCP?

I feel this would work better as a tale than as an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to agree with Anaxagoras here, in that it seems very similar to 884. Either the CI wants to Foundation to think she's important when she isn't so we waste resources (which probably isn't much more than they would've spent on her already), or she is important and the CI is acting stupid. It feels like they've tried this gambit before, and the Foundation should know better by now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed it, I really don't get why people are acting like this is a really common theme on the site, I can't even think of a single skip off the top of my head this is overly similar to.
Feel free to correct me though, but still a definite +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is something here. Give me a day or two, and I'll find out who or what Sarah Gruen/Grunwald is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it in chat, like it here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I'm also unsure if this should be an SCP or a tale or something else, but I like it anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with Djoric. This is has good tone, but doesn't do anything interesting. It has this scuffle between the CI and the Foundation, over someone who is just about as a "normal" person you can get inside the Foundation, but, despite the CI's interests, you give us little reason why they want her. You have an O5 say the obvious "we can't take the risk" that we are all expecting, and the ever unhelpful "believe what you want" response from the captured CI Task Force guy. Is there no way that you could have given us any clue to what she does? The closest we get is "she's not human."

Then whoop-dee-do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually enjoy the type of uncertainty and conflict these type of articles strive to elicit (I'm thinking about Samothrace and an SCP who's number I cannot remember; It was a camera that made some people look like aliens). However, like Anax, I just can't really see this being an SCP specifically. Kinda conflicted, so no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if I buy this as an SCP. It's events related to anomalies, but nothing here has any indication of anomalous stuff. Not even the CI agent says she's actually anomalous. The Foundation is allowed to lock up stuff other than anomalies.

That's not all the article's flaws, though. The last note, as has been said, is silly and detracts. If the girl is something special, the CI are idiots for trying to snatch her in the middle of a Foundation site. If she's not anything special, then it reads like SCP-884, except instead of the CI crippling thousands of Foundation personnel, they highly inconvenience one. This doesn't really make sense, either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then whoop-dee-do. I'm honestly incredibly tired of the "hey this thing/person is completely normal OR IS IT?" trope: I'm jaded to it to the point where I see it as lazy more than anything else. Sure, the question is there, but I will answer it with "I don't care". It's an empty space where something interesting could be, but isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also have a slight issue with -2 (taking in count this is not a unique anomaly, but a weaponized anomaly and technicallynot related to the main skip… Wich in this case is a perfectly normal girl). Honestly, I liked the premise, and I upvoted, but it needs more cohesion and fix that last note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels cruel.
Take your damn upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's interesting that she's a skip not because of any inherent anomaly but because she's a person of interest for a GoI. And the Foundation is just scratching their heads going, "But why?" Granted, I'm not sure if SCP classification is really warranted. I mean, was it done because "she might be anomalous" or because we can protect a skip from the CI better than a researcher? I wanna see some discussion of this, this is a very unique approach and is no doubt going to be controversial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or is it a perfectly normal individual? It's only as far as the Foundation can tell that Sarah is normal. But what if she's not, and the researcher at the end is wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enjoyed it as a draft, still like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with this. The last note is dumb and feels clumsy attempt to wring last minute feels

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is awesome. I was bracing for a twist but it never came, which is better that way because imagination almost always trumps "oh and she's actually X, boo!". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the conflict of interest bit at the end was tacked on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, and the concept after the hook was okay. It was saved for me due to the ambiguity of it all.

I really approve of the Foundation not knowing every last bit about their objects, it's more believable to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written but boring. You straddle the fence on what she is, which sucks the interest out of it. One way or the other, the article needs to make up its mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's just… boring, which it shouldn't be, due to the fact that there are CI transhuman operators here.

Only real traces hook I'm picking up here is that the CI is just fucking with the Foundation by going after a perfectly normal individual, and this is a trope I tend to really dislike in scips, the "the primary scip is something that's perfectly normal but THERE'S A TWIST". It weakens the imagery, which weakens the article overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved it in chat, loved it here. +1
